name: Modal Verbs
summary: Express can, want, will, and should with 会, 能, 可以, 要, 想, and 应该.
explanation:
Modal verbs sit before the main verb and add meaning like ability, permission, or desire. The key ones are 会 (huì, know how to / will), 能 (néng, be able to), 可以 (kěyǐ, may / be allowed), 要 (yào, want / must), 想 (xiǎng, would like), and 应该 (yīnggāi, should).

There is a useful split for 'can': 会 is a learned skill (我会游泳, I can swim), while 能 is physical possibility or capability right now (我今天不能来, I can't come today), and 可以 is permission (你可以走了, you may go).

To negate, put 不 before the modal: 我不能去 (I can't go). Most modals are negated with 不, except 想 and 要 which can also pair with 不.

examples.en: I can speak Chinese. (learned skill: 会)
Can you help me? (ability now: 能)
May I sit here? (permission: 可以)
I would like to drink coffee. (想)

commonMistakes: Using 会 for physical ability today: use 能 for 'able right now', 会 for a learned skill.
Conjugating or changing the modal. Like all Chinese verbs, modals never change form.
Putting the modal after the main verb. It always comes before.
Using 可以 when you mean ability rather than permission. 可以 is mainly 'allowed to'.
Forgetting that 想 (would like) is softer than 要 (want / must).
tip: For 'can', ask which kind: learned skill (会), able right now (能), or allowed to (可以). Picking the right one is most of the battle.
